column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=The station was Chalk River, and the train had waited forty minutes.
#Text=What\r
#Text=a comfortable feeling to know that all my belongings were safely on board!
#Text=I had not only saved time and money but an interesting trip across the\r
#Text=continent lay before me.
#Text=Having washed and put on clean garments, I had my\r
#Text=breakfast while passing through an enchanting hilly country, amid smiling\r
#Text=white birches, and the maples in the autumn glory of their foliage, with\r
#Text=more intensely red colouring than can be seen outside North America.
#Text=The\r
#Text=oatmeal porridge seemed unusually well prepared: the waiter intimated that\r
#Text=the cook was a Parisian.
#Text=However that might have been, he was probably of\r
#Text=French descent.
#Text=One day native honey was brought in, which had been found in a hollow\r
#Text=tree.
#Text=It was sweet, but thin, and had no pronounced flavour.
#Text=A few minutes\r
#Text=after the honey had been left on a plate in my tent there arrived a number\r
#Text=of large yellow hornets, quite harmless apparently, but persevering in\r
#Text=their eagerness to feast upon the honey.
#Text=During the foggy afternoon they\r
#Text=gathered in increased numbers and were driven off with difficulty.
#Text=The\r
#Text=temporary removal of the plate failed to diminish their persistence until\r
#Text=finally, at dusk, they disappeared, only to return again in the morning,\r
#Text=bringing others much larger in size and more vicious in aspect, and the\r
#Text=remaining sweet was consumed with incredible rapidity; in less than two\r
#Text=hours a considerable quantity of the honey in the comb as well as liquid\r
#Text=was finished by no great number of hornets.
#Text=Long Pangian is a small settlement where ten native soldiers are kept,\r
#Text=under the command of a so-called posthouder, in this case a civilized\r
#Text=Dayak from the South, who met us at the landing in an immaculate white\r
#Text=suit and new tan shoes.
#Text=It was warmer here toward the end of March than at\r
#Text=Tandjong Selor, because there had not been much rain for a month.
#Text=The soil\r
#Text=was therefore hard, and in the middle of the day so heated that after a\r
#Text=shower it remained as dry as before.
#Text=A few Chinamen and Bugis who live\r
#Text=here advance rice and dried fish to the Malays to provision expeditions\r
#Text=into the utan which last two to three months, receiving in return rubber\r
#Text=and damar.
#Text=The Malays come from lower down on the river, and a good many\r
#Text=of them leave their bones in the jungle, dying from beri-beri; others ill\r
#Text=with the same disease are barely able to return to Long Pangian, but in\r
#Text=three weeks those who do return usually recover sufficiently to walk about\r
#Text=again by adopting a diet of katsjang idju, the famous green peas of the\r
#Text=East Indies, which counteract the disease.
#Text=The Malays mix native\r
#Text=vegetables with them and thus make a kind of stew.
#Text=The rice traded in Borneo is of the ordinary polished variety, almost\r
#Text=exclusively from Rangoon, and it is generally supposed that the polishing\r
#Text=of the rice is the cause of this illness.
#Text=The Dutch army in the East seems\r
#Text=to have obtained good results by providing the so-called silver-fleeced\r
#Text=rice to the soldiers.
#Text=However, I was told that, in some localities at\r
#Text=least, the order had to be rescinded, because the soldiers objected so\r
#Text=strongly to that kind of rice.
#Text=Later, on this same river, I personally\r
#Text=experienced a swelling of the ankles, with an acceleration of the heart\r
#Text=action, which, on my return to Java, was pronounced by a medical authority\r
#Text=to be beri-beri.
#Text=Without taking any medicine, but simply by the changed\r
#Text=habits of life, with a variety of good food, the symptoms soon\r
#Text=disappeared.
#Text=It is undoubtedly true that the use of polished rice is a cause of\r
#Text=beri-beri, because the Dayaks, with their primitive methods of husking,\r
#Text=never suffer from this disease, although rice is their staple food.
#Text=Only on\r
#Text=occasions when members of these tribes take part in expeditions to New\r
#Text=Guinea, or are confined in prisons, and eat the rice offered of\r
#Text=civilization, are they afflicted with this malady.
#Text=In my own case I am\r
#Text=inclined to think that my indisposition at the commencement of my travels\r
#Text=in Borneo was largely due to the use of oatmeal from which the husks had\r
#Text=been removed.
#Text=Rolled oats is the proper food.
#Text=Modern research has established beyond doubt, that the outer layers of\r
#Text=grains contain mineral salts and vitamines that are indispensable to human\r
#Text=life.
#Text=Facts prove that man, if confined to an exclusive diet of white\r
#Text=bread, ultimately dies from malnutrition.
#Text=Cereals which have been\r
#Text="refined" of their husks present a highly starchy food, and unless they\r
#Text=are properly balanced by base-forming substances, trouble is sure to\r
#Text=follow.
#Text=Scurvy, beri-beri, and acidosis have been fatal to many\r
#Text=expeditions, though these diseases no doubt can be avoided by a judicious\r
#Text=selection of provisions that insure acid and base forming nutrition in the\r
#Text=right proportion. [*]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[Footnote *: For an illuminating example of poorly balanced food, see\r
#Text=_Physical Culture Magazine_, New York, for August, 1918, in which Mr.
#Text=Alfred W.
#Text=McCann describes the disaster to the Madeira-Mamore Railway\r
#Text=Company in Brazil, when "four thousand men were literally starved to death\r
#Text=on a white bread diet."
#Text=In the July number may be found the same food\r
#Text=expert's interesting manner of curing the crew of the German raider\r
#Text=_Kronprinz Wilhelm_, which in April, 1915, put in at Newport News, in\r
#Text=Virginia, with over a hundred men seriously stricken with acidosis.
#Text=The\r
#Text=crew had enjoyed an abundance of food from the ships they had raided and\r
#Text=destroyed, but a mysterious disease, pronounced to be beri-beri, was\r
#Text=crippling the crew.
#Text=As the patients failed to respond to the usual\r
#Text=treatment, the ship's chief surgeon consented to try the alkaline treatment\r
#Text=which Mr.
#Text=McCann suggested to him.
#Text=The patients rapidly recovered on a diet\r
#Text=consisting of fresh vegetable soup, potato-skin liquor, wheat bran,\r
#Text=whole-wheat bread, egg yolks, whole milk, orange juice, and apples.
#Text=No\r
#Text=drugs were administered.
#Text=It may be added that Dr.
#Text=Alfred Berg (in the same magazine, September,\r
#Text=1919) recounts the cure of an absolutely hopeless case of stomach trouble\r
#Text=by the vegetable juice prepared according to McCann's formula.
#Text=He has\r
#Text=found the results gained by the use of this soup in diet "so remarkable as\r
#Text=to be almost unbelievable."
#Text=The formula in question, as taken from McCann's article, is: "Boil\r
#Text=cabbage, carrots, parsnips, spinich, onions, turnips together for two\r
#Text=hours.

#Text=However, it helped me\r

#Text=The Kenyahs take turns" helping each other to harvest, and on this\r

column1: #Text=occasion they were assisting their chief.
#Text=It was a scene of much\r
#Text=animation, as if it were a festival, which in reality the harvesting is to\r
#Text=them.
#Text=The long row of men and women in their best garments, with\r
#Text=picturesque sun-shades, cut the spikes one by one, as the custom is, with\r
#Text=small knives held in the hollow of their hands.
#Text=Assuredly the food which\r
#Text=they received was tempting to hungry souls.
#Text=The rice, after being cooked,\r
#Text=was wrapped in banana leaves, one parcel for each, forty-four in all, and\r
#Text=as many more containing dried fish which also had been boiled.
#Text=The people\r
#Text=kindly acceded to my request to have them photographed.
#Text=They then packed\r
#Text=the harvested paddi in big baskets, which they carried on their backs to\r
#Text=the storehouse in the kampong the same afternoon.
#Text=From planting time till\r
#Text=the end of the harvest--four or five months--a man is deputed to remain in\r
#Text=the kampong to whom fish is forbidden, but who may eat all the rice he\r
#Text=wants, with some salt, and as recompense for his services receives a new\r
#Text=prahu or clothing.
#Text=In the morning, after a fine bath, as I was about to take breakfast, a\r
#Text=large party of visitors from Long Mahan approached.
#Text=They were unacquainted\r
#Text=with the Malay tongue and showed obvious signs of embarrassment, but by\r
#Text=distributing a little candy to the children and biscuits to the adults\r
#Text=harmony was soon established.
#Text=Two unusually attractive small girls wearing\r
#Text=valuable bead necklaces, who at first had appeared takut (frightened),\r
#Text=unconcernedly seated themselves on their heels in front of me.
#Text=The others\r
#Text=perched in a long row on two poles which they laid on the wet ground, all\r
#Text=of them preparing to watch me eat breakfast.
#Text=Among other things the menu\r
#Text=included half a dozen small boiled potatoes brought from Tandjong Selor\r
#Text=and obtained from Central Java; they usually keep for four or five weeks\r
#Text=and are a valuable aid in maintaining good health in the tropics.
#Text=The Kenyahs had never seen potatoes before, and one man handed some of the\r
#Text=peelings to his wife for inspection, whereupon I gave her a potato, which\r
#Text=she peeled carefully, divided, and gave a piece to each of the two\r
#Text=children, with whom, however, it did not find favour.
#Text=I opened a can of\r
#Text=milk and another of cream, for I was fresh from Europe and had plenty of\r
#Text=provisions.
#Text=After helping myself from the cans I gave them to the\r
#Text=children, who greatly relished what was left in them, but they did not eat\r
#Text=greedily, behaving like white children who have not learned from adults to\r
#Text=eat hastily.
#Text=The Kenyahs are very courteous.
#Text=When a man passed my tent\r
#Text=opening he generally called aloud, as if announcing his presence.
#Text=In visiting the camps I found the Kenyahs, even on an occasion like the\r
#Text=present, busily engaged at some occupation, and seldom or never was\r
#Text=anybody seen sitting idle.
#Text=The men were splitting rattan into fine\r
#Text=strings, later to be used for many purposes: for plaiting the sheath of\r
#Text=the parang; for making bottle-shaped receptacles for rice; for securing\r
#Text=the axe to the handle, etc.
#Text=Women were doing the same work with bamboo,\r
#Text=first drying the stalks by standing them upright before a fire.
#Text=These fine\r
#Text=bamboo strings are later used in making winnowing trays and for various\r
#Text=kinds of beautifully plaited work.
#Text=When employed in this way, or on other\r
#Text=occasions, the women smoke big cigarettes as nonchalantly as the men.
#Text=On the river bank, as we approached the main camping-place, piles of the\r
#Text=light-brown root were often seen, resembling stacks of wood.
#Text=The gathering\r
#Text=of these roots, I learned, was accomplished in one day.
#Text=Our men had helped\r
#Text=in the work and they also put up a couple of "bring" near our camp for our\r
#Text=own use.
#Text=Early in the afternoon two rather solid structures, built like\r
#Text=bridges across the small river, were erected; on these the beating of the\r
#Text=tuba was to take place next morning.
#Text=In the middle, lengthwise, was placed\r
#Text=a long, narrow excavated log, longer than the bridge itself, for the use\r
#Text=of the beaters.
#Text=In the evening a large tree crashed to earth not far from my camp, and at\r
#Text=a later hour another, still nearer, thunderously broke with its fall the\r
#Text=silence of night.
#Text=At two o'clock in the morning the beating of tuba began,\r
#Text=to the accompaniment of shouts and outcries, and though the noise was\r
#Text=considerable and unusual I did not find it intolerable, but fell asleep\r
#Text=again.
#Text=I arose early, and after partaking of some excellent Dayak rice I\r
#Text=walked down to view the proceedings, and found the scene engrossing.
#Text=Men\r
#Text=and women stood close together on each side of the long trough, crushing\r
#Text=the tuba with sticks in a similar manner to that adopted when pounding\r
#Text=rice.
#Text=The trough had at one end a small compartment, open like the rest,\r
#Text=but the sides had been smoothed with an axe and when beaten served the\r
#Text=purpose of a gong.
#Text=The bark was pounded into small pieces and then thrown\r
#Text=to one side upon large palm leaves which covered the bridge.
#Text=Boarding a prahu, I next visited Amban Klesau's bridge, a little lower\r
#Text=down, which was larger and more pretentious, with tall poles erected on\r
#Text=it, and from the top hung ornamental wood shavings.
#Text=The end of the trough\r
#Text=here had actually been carved into a semblance of the head of "an animal\r
#Text=which lives in the ground," probably representing a supernatural being\r
#Text=usually called nagah.
#Text=The owner himself was beating it with a stick on\r
#Text=both sides of the head, and this made more noise than the pounding of the\r
#Text=fifty men and women who stood working at the trough.
#Text=At times they walked\r
#Text=in single file around it.
#Text=The pounding was finished in the forenoon, and all went a little farther\r
#Text=down the river to take the fire omen at a place where the river widened\r
#Text=out into a pool.
#Text=A man with many tail-feathers from the rhinoceros\r
#Text=hornbill (_buceros rhinoceros_) stuck into his rattan cap seated himself\r
#Text=on a crude platform which had been built on upright poles over the water.
#Text=Some long pieces of tuba-root were lying there, and he squatted on his\r
#Text=heels facing the principal men who were sitting on the bank south of him.
#Text=The insignificant number of fish caught--nearly all of the same kind--was\r
#Text=surprising and disappointing.
#Text=Even small fish were eagerly sought.
#Text=There\r
#Text=was little animation, especially at the beginning of the sport, and no\r
#Text=spears were used.
#Text=Several tons of bark must have been utilized, at least\r
#Text=eight or ten times as much as at the Isau River, and I regretted that they\r
#Text=should have so little reward for their trouble.
#Text=Five days were spent in\r
#Text=travel, two days in making "bring" and gathering tuba, and they had\r
#Text=pounded tuba for eight hours, since two o'clock in the morning.
#Text=After all\r
#Text=these exertions many prahus must have returned without fish.
#Text=Possibly the\r
#Text=fish had been practically exterminated by the tuba poisoning of former\r
#Text=years.
#Text=One man told me that many fish remain dead at the bottom, which\r
#Text=partly accounts for the scanty result.
#Text=I was desirous of having Chonggat remain here for a week of collecting,\r
#Text=but no Kenyah was willing to stay with him, all being deterred through\r
#Text=fear of Punan head-hunters, who, on this river, not so long ago, had\r
#Text=killed some rubber-gatherers from Sarawak.
#Text=Besides, they also anticipated\r
#Text=revenge on the part of Kayans, eleven of whom had been killed by the\r
#Text=Kenyahs in Apo Kayan one and a half years previously.
#Text=According to their\r
#Text=own reports and that of the Chinese interpreter, the heads of six men and\r
#Text=five women had been taken after a successful attack on the two prahus in\r
#Text=which the Kayans (Oma-Lakan) travelled.
#Text=The Kenyahs (Oma-Kulit) who had\r
#Text=committed the outrage had been apprehended by the Company, as the\r
#Text=government is called by the natives.
#Text=The brother of the chief of Long\r
#Text=Pelaban, who was with us fishing, three months previously had returned\r
#Text=from Samarinda, where he had spent one year in prison for having been\r
#Text=implicated in a minor way in this crime, while the main offenders were\r
#Text=serving labor terms of six years in Sorabaia, Java.
#Text=In Tandjong Selor I was exceedingly busy for three days getting boxes and\r
#Text=packing the collections, and early in June I departed for Bandjermasin, on\r
#Text=S.S.
#Text=_De Weert_.
#Text=It has been my fortune to travel much on the steamships\r
#Text=of the Royal Packet Boat Company, which controls the whole Malay\r
#Text=Archipelago from Singapore to New Guinea and the Moluccas.
#Text=It is always a\r
#Text=pleasure to board one of these steamers, as the officers are invariably\r
#Text=courteous, and the food is as excellent on the smaller steamers as on the\r
#Text=large ones.
#Text=The same kind of genuine, good claret, at a reasonable price,\r
#Text=is also found on all of them, and it may readily be understood how much I\r
#Text=enjoyed a glass of cool Margaux-Medoc with dinner, after over five months\r
#Text=in the utan.
#Text=The sailors on these steamers are Javanese.
#Text=Those from\r
#Text=Madura, rather small men, made an especially good impression.
#Text=A captain\r
#Text=told me they never give any trouble except when on leave ashore in\r
#Text=Sourabaia, where they occasionally remain overtime, but after a few days\r
#Text=they come to the office and want to be taken on again.
#Text=They are punished\r
#Text=by having their wages deducted for the days they are absent, but the loss\r
#Text=of coin does not trouble them much.
#Text=If they have cigarettes and their\r
#Text=meals they are happy, and they never accumulate money.
#Text=They are engaged\r
#Text=for one year and some of them renew their contracts.
#Text=As we sailed southward from the Kayan River we were told of a French count\r
#Text=who with his wife lived on an island three or four kilometres long, near\r
#Text=the coast.
#Text=At first he had fisheries and sold dried fish, which, with\r
#Text=rice, forms the staple food of the natives of Borneo and other countries\r
#Text=of the East.
#Text=He was enabled to change his business into cocoanut\r
#Text=plantations, which to-day cover the island.
#Text=According to report they\r
#Text=dressed for dinner every day, to the end that they might not relinquish\r
#Text=their hold upon the habits of civilised society.
#Text=Later I learned that when\r
#Text=the war broke out the count immediately went to France to offer his\r
#Text=services.
#Text=In the Malay kampong, Pembuang, I procured a large pomelo, in Borneo\r
#Text=called limao, a delicious juicy fruit of the citrus order, but light-pink\r
#Text=inside and with little or no acidity.
#Text=After the exertions of the night\r
#Text=this, together with canned bacon, fried and boiled potatoes, furnished an\r
#Text=ideal midday meal.
#Text=Necessary repairs having been made to the engine, next\r
#Text=day, on a charming, peaceful afternoon, we continued our trip up the\r
#Text=river.
#Text=An unusually large number of monkeys were seen on both sides, and\r
#Text=the men sat on the railing, with their feet hanging outside, to look at\r
#Text=them.
#Text=The red, long-nosed variety did not retreat, but looked at us calmly\r
#Text=from the branch where it sat; other species hurried off, making incredibly\r
#Text=long leaps from branch to branch.
#Text=Shortly after sunset we threw anchor.
#Text=On returning from our walk, near sunset, I asked the kapala how much I had\r
#Text=to pay for the bringing ashore of my baggage.
#Text="Fifteen rupia" (florins)\r
#Text=was the answer.
#Text=As things go in Borneo this was an incredibly excessive\r
#Text=charge, and as my intention was to go by boat to the Dayak kampong on the\r
#Text=lake, and from there march overland to the small river, Kuala Sampit, I\r
#Text=demanded to know how much then I would have to pay for twenty men that I\r
#Text=needed for the journey.
#Text="Five rupia a day for each," he said.
#Text=Dayaks, who\r
#Text=are far more efficient and reliable, are satisfied with one rupia a day.
#Text=Those near by protested that it was not too much, because in gathering\r
#Text=rubber they made even more a day.
#Text=At that rate it would have cost me a\r
#Text=hundred florins a day, besides their food, with the prospects of having\r
#Text=strikes for higher pay all the way, according to the Malay custom.
#Text=A kindly antoh may enter a man and become his guardian spirit, to whom he\r
#Text=occasionally offers food, but it never remains long because that would\r
#Text=make the man insane.
#Text=One must not step over a person, because a benevolent\r
#Text=antoh that may be in possession is liable to be frightened away, say the\r
#Text=Katingans and other Dayaks.
#Text=In dancing with masks, which is much practised\r
#Text=on the Mahakam, the idea is that the antoh of the animal represented by\r
#Text=the mask enters the dancer through the top of his head.
#Text=The Penihings and Long-Glats of the Mahakam have an interesting belief in\r
#Text=the existence of a friendly antoh which reminded me of the superstition of\r
#Text=the "Nokken" in the rivers of Norway.
#Text=It lives in rivers, is very rarely\r
#Text=beheld by mortals, and the one who sees it becomes rich beyond dreams of\r
#Text=avarice.
#Text=The Long-Glats call it sangiang, a survival of Hindu influence.
#Text=An old man in Long Tujo is reported to have seen this antoh, and according\r
#Text=to him it had the appearance of a woman sitting underneath the water.
#Text=No\r
#Text=doubt other tribes have the same belief.
#Text=The most famous of antohs is the nagah, which may be good or evil,\r
#Text=according to the treatment received from mortals, and being very powerful\r
#Text=its help and protection are sought in a manner later to be described in\r
#Text=connection with my travels on the Mahakam.
#Text=The nagah guards underneath as\r
#Text=well as above the surface of water and earth, but the air is protected by\r
#Text=three birds which are messengers, or mail carriers, so to speak.
#Text=They are\r
#Text=able to call the good antoh and carry food to him; they are also\r
#Text=attendants of man and watch over him and his food.
#Text=Fowls and pigs are\r
#Text=sacrificed to them as payment.
#Text=They are--the tingang (hornbill), the\r
#Text=sankuvai (formerly on earth but now only in heaven), and the antang (red\r
#Text=hawk).
#Text=As these birds are called by the same names in the tribes of the\r
#Text=Katingans, Ot-Danums, Kahayans, and others, it may be presumed that their\r
#Text=worship is widely prevalent in Borneo.
#Text=A Murung one day brought and exhibited to us that extraordinary animal,\r
#Text=the scaly ant-eater (_manis_), which is provided with a long pipe-like\r
#Text=snout, and is devoid of teeth because its only food, the ant, is gathered\r
#Text=by means of its long tongue.
#Text=The big scales that cover the whole body form\r
#Text=its sole defence, and when it rolls itself up the dogs can do it no harm.
#Text=Unable to run, it cannot even walk fast, and the long tail is held\r
#Text=straight out without touching the ground.
#Text=Its appearance directs one's\r
#Text=thoughts back to the monsters of prehistoric times, and the fat meat is\r
#Text=highly esteemed by the Dayaks.
#Text=The animal, which is possessed of\r
#Text=incredible strength in proportion to its size, was put in a box from which\r
#Text=it escaped in the night through the carelessness of Rajimin.
#Text=A large live porcupine was also brought for sale by a Dayak woman who had\r
#Text=raised it.
#Text=The creature was confined in a kind of bag, and by means of its\r
#Text=strength it managed to escape from between the hands of the owner.
#Text=Although she and several Dayaks immediately started in pursuit, it\r
#Text=succeeded in eluding them.
#Text=However, the woman believed implicitly that it\r
#Text=would return, and a couple of days later it did reappear, passing my tent\r
#Text=at dusk.
#Text=Every evening afterward about eight o'clock it was a regular\r
#Text=visitor, taking food out of my hand and then continuing its trip to the\r
#Text=kitchen, which was less than a hundred metres farther up the river bank.
#Text=Finally it became a nuisance, turning over saucepans to look for food and\r
#Text=otherwise annoying us, so I bought it for one ringit in order to have it\r
#Text=skinned.
#Text=The difficulty was to catch it, because its quills are long and\r
#Text=sharp; but next evening the Murungs brought it to me enmeshed in a strong\r
#Text=net, and how to kill it was the next question.
#Text=My Malay cook was taken ill, so I had to do most of the cooking myself,\r
#Text=which is not particularly pleasant when one's time is valuable; and when\r
#Text=he got well his lack of experience rendered it necessary for me to oversee\r
#Text=his culinary operations.
#Text=One day after returning to my tent from such\r
#Text=supervision I had a curious adventure with a snake.
#Text=It was a warm day\r
#Text=about half past one.
#Text=All was quiet and not a blade stirred.
#Text=I paused near\r
#Text=the tent opening, with my face toward the opposite side of the river,\r
#Text=which could be seen through an opening among the trees.
#Text=Standing\r
#Text=motionless on the bank, which from there sloped gradually down toward the\r
#Text=river, more than a minute had elapsed when my attention was distracted by\r
#Text=a slight noise behind me.
#Text=Looking to the right and backward my surprise\r
#Text=was great to perceive the tail-end of a black snake rapidly proceeding\r
#Text=toward the left.
#Text=Hastily turning my eyes in that direction I beheld the\r
#Text=well-shaped, powerful, though somewhat slender, forward part of the\r
#Text=serpent, which, holding its head high, almost to the height of my knee,\r
#Text=made downward toward the river.
#Text=When a person dies there is much wailing, and if the deceased is a father\r
#Text=or mother people of the same house do not sleep for three days.
#Text=The corpse\r
#Text=remains in the house three days, during which time a root called javau is\r
#Text=eaten instead of rice, babi and bananas being also permissible.
#Text=The body\r
#Text=is washed and wrapped in white cotton cloth, bought from Malay traders,\r
#Text=and placed in a coffin made of iron-wood.
#Text=As the coffin must not be\r
#Text=carried through the door, the house wall is broken open for it to pass on\r
#Text=its way to a cemetery in the utan.
#Text=Sometimes as soon as one year\r
#Text=afterward, but usually much later, the coffin is opened, the bones are\r
#Text=cleaned with water and soap and placed in a new box of the same material\r
#Text=or in a gutshi, an earthen jar bought from the Chinese.
#Text=The box or jar is\r
#Text=then deposited in a subterranean chamber made of iron-wood, called kobur\r
#Text=by both Malays and Murungs, where in addition are left the personal\r
#Text=effects of the deceased,--clothing, beads, and other ornaments,--and, if a\r
#Text=man, also his sumpitan, parang, axe, etc.
#Text=This disposition of the bones is\r
#Text=accompanied by a very elaborate feast, generally called tiwah, to the\r
#Text=preparation of which much time is devoted.
#Text=On Christmas day I bought from an old Dayak a large, ripe fruit called in\r
#Text=Malay nangca (_artocarpus integrifolia_) of the jack fruit family.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=very common.
#Text=Before maturing it is used as an every-day vegetable, which\r
#Text=is boiled before eating.
#Text=I was surprised to find that when fully ripe this\r
#Text=fruit has an agreeable flavour of banana, but its contents being sticky it\r
#Text=is difficult to eat.
#Text=The sergeant, with the culinary ability of the\r
#Text=Javanese, prepared for the holiday a kind of stew, called sambil goreng,\r
#Text=which is made on the same principle as the Mexican variety, but decidedly\r
#Text=superior.
#Text=Besides the meat or fish, or whatever is used as the foundation,\r
#Text=it contains eight ingredients and condiments, all indigenous except red\r
#Text=pepper and onions.
#Text=In the ladangs is cultivated the maize plant, which just then was in\r
#Text=condition to provide us with the coveted green corn, and carried my\r
#Text=thoughts to America, whence the plant came.
#Text=Maize is raised on a very\r
#Text=limited scale, and, strange to say, higher up the river the season was\r
#Text=already over.
#Text=At Poru we tried in vain to secure a kind of gibbon that we\r
#Text=heard almost daily on the other side of the river, emitting a loud cry but\r
#Text=different from that of the ordinary wah-wah.
#Text=Rajimin described it as being\r
#Text=white about the head and having a pronounced kind of topknot.
#Text=No cooking utensils were employed.
#Text=Sago was wrapped in leaves and placed\r
#Text=on the fire, and the meat was roasted.
#Text=There is no cooking separately for\r
#Text=men and women, and meals are taken irregularly, but usually twice a day.
#Text=The crocodile is not eaten, because it would make one mad, nor are\r
#Text=domestic dogs or omen birds used for food.
#Text=Honey is collected by cutting\r
#Text=down the tree.
#Text=Their principal weapon is the sumpitan, which, as usual,\r
#Text=with a spear point lashed to one end, also serves as spear and is bought\r
#Text=from the Saputans.
#Text=Parang and shield complete the man's outfit.
#Text=On the\r
#Text=Busang only ten ipoh (upas) trees are known from which poison may be\r
#Text=obtained for the blow-pipe darts; to get a new supply a journey of two\r
#Text=days down the river is necessary, and six for the return.
#Text=A pregnant woman must not eat durian which, in falling from the tree, has\r
#Text=broken, or stuck in a cleft without reaching the ground, nor any kind of\r
#Text=fruit that does not fall straight to earth, nor sago from a palm tree\r
#Text=which chanced to become entangled by a branch instead of falling directly\r
#Text=to the ground, nor the large hornbill, nor snakes, nor pigs, nor fish that\r
#Text=were killed by being struck on the head, or by any other means than with\r
#Text=spear or parang, nor land turtle, nor the scaly ant-eater.
#Text=She must not\r
#Text=make a house or take part in making it, and therefore if a pole has to be\r
#Text=put in place she must call another woman to do it.
#Text=Further, she must not eat an animal which has lost one or both eyes, nor\r
#Text=one the foot of which has been crushed, nor an animal of strong odour\r
#Text=(like civet cat, skunk, etc., not an offensive smell to these natives);\r
#Text=nor are she and her husband permitted to gather rubber, nor may wood be\r
#Text=gathered for fire-making which has roads on it made by ants.
#Text=She must not\r
#Text=drink water from a back current, nor water which runs through a fallen\r
#Text=tree.
#Text=A pig may be eaten, but if it has a foetus inside that must be\r
#Text=avoided.
#Text=The husband also observes all these tabus and precautions.
#Text=A tree is cut down and from the log a dugout is made in which the corpse\r
#Text=is placed, a board being loosely fastened as a cover.
#Text=This coffin is\r
#Text=placed on a simple platform in the utan.
#Text=There is no feast attending this\r
#Text=rite.
#Text=I visited the burial-place (taaran) of Tamaloe on the other side of\r
#Text=the river about a kilometre away.
#Text=It was difficult to find, for the small\r
#Text=space which is cleared of jungle whenever there is a funeral very soon\r
#Text=grows up again.
#Text=Only two boxes, each containing the corpse of a child,\r
#Text=were in good condition, the rest having fallen down and disappeared\r
#Text=through the action of rains and wild pigs.
#Text=After the husband's death the widow eats only every second day for a\r
#Text=month; after that she is free to eat, but for a year she weeps twice a\r
#Text=day, morning and evening,--though sometimes she forgets.
#Text=The father,\r
#Text=mother, and sister of the deceased also take part in the one-year period\r
#Text=of wailing twice a day.
#Text=After that period has elapsed the widow may\r
#Text=remarry.
#Text=For the widower there are practically the same regulations,\r
#Text=though he does not weep loudly, and after eight months he can look for\r
#Text=another wife; but first he must have taken a head.
#Text=Wild pigs and deer continued plentiful, but the monkeys seemed gradually\r
#Text=to disappear.
#Text=Fish there were in plenty, but they were now of smaller\r
#Text=kinds, not agreeable to eat, having an oily taste and mostly very bony.
#Text=At\r
#Text=all our camping places ants of various kinds were numerous, also inside of\r
#Text=the tent, but they did not seem to be obnoxious.
#Text=Just before sunset the\r
#Text=loud voices of the cicadas began, and after dark lovely moths were\r
#Text=attracted by my lamp, while during the night bats flew in and out of my\r
#Text=tent.
#Text=The humidity of the atmosphere was great.
#Text=Safety matches would not\r
#Text=strike fire unless kept in an airtight box.
#Text=My cameras were inside of\r
#Text=solid steel boxes, provided with rubber bands against the covers, making\r
#Text=them water-tight.
#Text=Nevertheless, upon opening one that had been closed for\r
#Text=three weeks the camera inside was found to be white with mould.
#Text=The men and the prahus came promptly and we began loading; I was glad at\r
#Text=the prospect of getting away from the low-lying country, where we had our\r
#Text=camp among bamboo trees, with the chance of being flooded should the river\r
#Text=rise too high.
#Text=As we were standing near my tent, getting ready to take it\r
#Text=down, a plandok (mouse-deer, _tragulus_) came along--among the Saputans,\r
#Text=and probably most Dayaks, reputed to be the wisest and most cunning of all\r
#Text=animals, and in folklore playing the part of our fox.
#Text=It was conspicuously\r
#Text=pregnant and passed unconcernedly just back of the tent.
#Text=As the flesh is a\r
#Text=favourite food of both Dayaks and Malays they immediately gave chase,\r
#Text=shouting and trying to surround it, which made the plandok turn back; then\r
#Text=the wonderfully agile Saputan chief darted after it and actually caught it\r
#Text=alive.
#Text=Extraordinary agility is characteristic of most Dayaks.
#Text=An army\r
#Text=officer in his report of the Katingans describes how a Dayak "suddenly\r
#Text=jumped overboard, drew his parang, and with one stroke cut a fish through\r
#Text=the middle.
#Text=there would not be many children; the paddi would not ripen well, or would\r
#Text=fail; wild beasts would eat the fowls, and there would be no bananas or\r
#Text=other fruits."
#Text=The first four days are chiefly taken up with preparations,\r
#Text=the festival occurring on the fifth and sixth days.
#Text=A place of worship\r
#Text=adjoining the front of the easternmost house was being constructed, with a\r
#Text=floor high above ground on a level with the gallery, with which it was\r
#Text=connected by a couple of planks for a bridge.
#Text=Although flimsily built, the\r
#Text=structure was abundantly strong to support the combined weight of the\r
#Text=eight female blians who at times performed therein.
#Text=The hut, which was\r
#Text=profusely decorated with long, hanging wood shavings, is called dangei and\r
#Text=is an important adjunct of the feast, to which the same name is sometimes\r
#Text=given.
#Text=Ordinary people are not allowed to enter, though they may ascend\r
#Text=the ladder, giving access to the gallery, in close proximity to the\r
#Text=sanctuary.
#Text=Prior to the fifth day a progressive scale is observed in regard to food\r
#Text=regulations, and after the sixth, when the festive high mark is reached,\r
#Text=there is a corresponding decrease to normal.
#Text=Only a little boiled rice is\r
#Text=eaten the first day, but on the second, third, and fourth, rations are\r
#Text=gradually increased by limited additions of toasted rice.
#Text=The fifth and\r
#Text=sixth days give occasion for indulgence in much rice and pork, the\r
#Text=quantity being reduced on the seventh, when the remaining pork is\r
#Text=finished.
#Text=On the eighth and ninth days the regulations permit only boiled\r
#Text=and toasted rice.
#Text=Not much food remains on the tenth, when the menu\r
#Text=reverts to boiled rice exclusively.
#Text=Some kinds of fish may be eaten during\r
#Text=the ten-day period, while others are prohibited.
#Text=This was followed by a dance of the blians present, nine or ten in number,\r
#Text=to the accompaniment of four gongs and one drum.
#Text=They moved in single\r
#Text=file, most of them making two steps and a slight turn to left, two steps\r
#Text=and a slight turn to right, while others moved straight on.
#Text=In this way\r
#Text=they described a drawn-out circle, approaching an ellipse, sixteen times.
#Text=After the dancing those who took part in the ceremonies ate toasted rice.
#Text=Each day of the feast in the afternoon food was given to antoh by blians\r
#Text=and girl pupils.
#Text=Boiled rice, a small quantity of salt, some dried fish,\r
#Text=and boiled fowl were wrapped in pieces of banana leaves, and two such\r
#Text=small parcels were offered on each occasion.
#Text=Meantime the festive preparations continued.
#Text=Many loads of bamboo were\r
#Text=brought in, because much rice and much pork was to be cooked in these\r
#Text=handy utensils provided by nature.
#Text=Visitors were slowly but steadily\r
#Text=arriving.
#Text=On the fourth day came the principal man, the Raja Besar (great\r
#Text=chief), who resides a little further up the river, accompanied by his\r
#Text=family.
#Text=The son of a Long-Glat father and an Oma-Suling mother, Ledjuli\r
#Text=claimed to be raja not only of these tribes, but also of the Kayans.
#Text=Next\r
#Text=morning Raja Besar and his stately wife, of Oma-Suling nobility,\r
#Text=accompanied by the kapala of the kampong and others, paid me a visit,\r
#Text=presenting me with a long sugarcane, a somewhat rare product in these\r
#Text=parts and considered a great delicacy, one large papaya, white onions, and\r
#Text=bananas.
#Text=In return I gave one cake of chocolate, two French tins of meat,\r
#Text=one tin of boiled ham, and tobacco.
#Text=About four o'clock in the afternoon the Dayaks began to kill the pigs by\r
#Text=cutting the artery of the neck.
#Text=The animals, which were in surprisingly\r
#Text=good condition, made little outcry.
#Text=The livers were examined, and if found\r
#Text=to be of bad omen were thrown away, but the pig itself is eaten in such\r
#Text=cases, though a full-grown fowl or a tiny chicken only a few days old must\r
#Text=be sacrificed in addition.
#Text=The carcasses were freed from hair by fire in\r
#Text=the usual way and afterward cleaned with the knife.
#Text=The skin is eaten with\r
#Text=the meat, which at night was cooked in bamboo.
#Text=Outside, in front of the\r
#Text=houses, rice cooking had been going on all day.
#Text=In one row there were\r
#Text=perhaps fifty bamboos, each stuffed with envelopes of banana leaves\r
#Text=containing rice, the parcels being some thirty centimetres long and three\r
#Text=wide.
#Text=During the night there was a grand banquet in all the houses.
#Text=Lidju, my\r
#Text=assistant, did not forget, on this day of plenty, to send my party\r
#Text=generous gifts of fresh pork.
#Text=To me he presented a fine small ham.
#Text=As salt\r
#Text=had been left behind we had to boil the meat à la Dayak in bamboo with\r
#Text=very little water, which compensates for the absence of seasoning.
#Text=A\r
#Text=couple of men brought us two bamboos containing that gelatinous delicacy\r
#Text=into which rice is transformed when cooked in this way.
#Text=And, as if this\r
#Text=were not enough, early next morning a procession arrived carrying food on\r
#Text=two shields, the inside being turned upward.
#Text=On these were parcels wrapped\r
#Text=in banana leaves containing boiled rice, to which were tied large pieces\r
#Text=of cooked pork.
#Text=The first man to appear stepped up to a banana growing\r
#Text=near, broke off a leaf which he put on the ground in front of me, and\r
#Text=placed on it two bundles.
#Text=The men were unable to speak Malay and\r
#Text=immediately went away without making even a suggestion that they expected\r
#Text=remuneration, as did the two who had given us rice.
#Text=I had never seen them\r
#Text=before.
#Text=The sixth day was one of general rejoicing.
#Text=Food was exchanged between the\r
#Text=two groups of houses and people were in a very joyful mood, eating pork,\r
#Text=running about, and playing tricks on each other.
#Text=Both men and women\r
#Text=carried charcoal mixed with the fat of pork, with which they tried to\r
#Text=smear the face and upper body of all whom they met.
#Text=All were privileged to\r
#Text=engage in this sport but the women were especially active, pursuing the\r
#Text=men, who tried to avoid them, some taking refuge behind my tent.
#Text=The women\r
#Text=followed one man through the enclosure surrounding the tent, at my\r
#Text=invitation, but they did not succeed in catching him.
#Text=This practical\r
#Text=joking was continued on the following days except the last.
#Text=The Oma-Palo had their own festival, which lasted only one day.
#Text=It began\r
#Text=in the afternoon of the sixth day and I went over to see it.
#Text=The livers of\r
#Text=the pigs were not in favourable condition, which caused much delay in the\r
#Text=proceedings, and it was nearly five o'clock when they finally began to\r
#Text=make a primitive dángei hut, all the material for which had been gathered.
#Text=A few slim upright poles with human faces carved at the upper ends were\r
#Text=placed so as to form the outline of a quadrangle.
#Text=On the ground between\r
#Text=them planks were laid, and on the two long sides of this space were raised\r
#Text=bamboo stalks with leaves on, which leaned together and formed an airy\r
#Text=cover.
#Text=It was profusely adorned with wood shavings hung by the ends in\r
#Text=long spirals, the whole arrangement forming a much simpler house of\r
#Text=worship than the one described above.
#Text=The kapala having sacrificed a tiny\r
#Text=chicken, a man performed a war dance on the planks in superb fashion, and\r
#Text=after that two female blians danced.
#Text=Next morning I returned and asked\r
#Text=permission to photograph the dancing.
#Text=The kapala replied that if a\r
#Text=photograph were made while they were working--that is to say,\r
#Text=dancing--they would have to do all their work over again, otherwise some\r
#Text=misfortune would come upon them, such as the falling of one of the bamboo\r
#Text=stalks, which might kill somebody.
#Text=Later, while they were eating, for\r
#Text=example, there would be no objection to the accomplishment of my desire.
#Text=Shortly afterward followed a unique performance of throwing rice, small\r
#Text=bundles of which, wrapped in banana leaves, were lying in readiness on the\r
#Text=floor.
#Text=Some of the men caught them with such violence that the rice was\r
#Text=spilled all about, and then they flipped the banana leaves at those who\r
#Text=stood near.
#Text=Some of the women had crawled up under the roof in\r
#Text=anticipation of what was coming.
#Text=After a few minutes passed thus, the\r
#Text=eight blians seated themselves in the dángei hut and prepared food for\r
#Text=antoh in the way described above, but on this occasion one of them pounded\r
#Text=paddi with two short bamboo sticks, singing all the while.
#Text=Having concluded their task of feeding the antohs the blians climbed down\r
#Text=the ladder and began a march in single file round the dángei hut, each\r
#Text=carrying one of the implements of daily life: a spear, a small parang, an\r
#Text=axe, an empty rattan bag in which the bamboos are enclosed when the woman\r
#Text=fetches water, or in which vegetables, etc., are conveyed, and another bag\r
#Text=of the same material suitable for transporting babi.
#Text=Four of the women\r
#Text=carried the small knife which is woman's special instrument, though also\r
#Text=employed by the men.
#Text=When the eight blians on this, the eighth day, had\r
#Text=marched sixteen times around the dángei they ascended the ladder again.
#Text=Shortly afterward a man standing on the gallery pushed over the flimsy\r
#Text=place of worship--a signal that the end of the feast had come.
#Text=On the\r
#Text=previous day a few visitors had departed and others left daily.
#Text=The feast had brought together from other parts about 200 Oma-Sulings and\r
#Text=Long-Glats.
#Text=The women of both tribes showed strikingly fine manners,\r
#Text=especially those belonging to the higher class, which was well\r
#Text=represented.
#Text=Some were expensively dressed, though in genuine barbaric\r
#Text=fashion as indicated by the ornaments sewn upon their skirts, which\r
#Text=consisted of hundreds of florins and ringits.
#Text=It should be conceded,\r
#Text=however, that with the innate artistic sense of the Dayaks, the coins, all\r
#Text=scrupulously clean, had been employed to best advantage in pretty designs,\r
#Text=and the damsels were strong enough to carry the extra burden.
#Text=The climax had been passed and little more was going on, the ninth day\r
#Text=being given over to the amusement of daubing each other with black paste.
#Text=On the tenth day they all went away to a small river in the neighbourhood,\r
#Text=where they took their meals, cooking paddi in bamboo, also fish in the\r
#Text=same manner.
#Text=This proceeding is called násam, and the pemáli (tabu) is now\r
#Text=all over.
#Text=During the days immediately following the people may go to the\r
#Text=ladang, but are obliged to sleep in the kampong, and they must not\r
#Text=undertake long journeys.
#Text=When the feast ended the blians placed four eggs\r
#Text=in the clefts of four upright bamboo sticks as sacrifice to antoh.
#Text=Such\r
#Text=eggs are gathered from hens that are sitting, and those which have become\r
#Text=stale in unoccupied nests are also used.
#Text=If there are not enough such\r
#Text=eggs, fresh ones are taken.
#Text=The head-hunters, who must take their food apart from their associates and\r
#Text=in the presence of the heads, now bring water from the river to boil rice,\r
#Text=in bamboo, outside on the gallery.
#Text=When the cooking is finished the heads\r
#Text=are brought to take part in the meal, being hung near the place where the\r
#Text=men are to eat and about half a metre above the floor, to be out of reach\r
#Text=of dogs.
#Text=A pinch of rice is put into the hole at the top of the skull and\r
#Text=the head is addressed in the following words: "Eat this rice first.
#Text=durians is a new sensation, worth a voyage to the East to experience."
#Text=There were some superb trees seventy metres high growing not far from my\r
#Text=tent, and many others farther away.
#Text=The people of the Mahakam do not climb\r
#Text=these tall trees to get the fruit, but gather them from the ground after\r
#Text=it has fallen.
#Text=One night I heard one fall with a considerable crash.
#Text=Roughly speaking, it is of the size of a cocoanut; a large one might kill\r
#Text=a man and has been known to cause serious injury.
#Text=It is most dangerous for\r
#Text=children to walk under the trees in the fruit season.
#Text=The durian is intensely appreciated by the natives, and tatu marks\r
#Text=representing the fruit are strikingly prominent in Central Borneo.
#Text=It also\r
#Text=has its European devotees, though most of them take a dislike to it on\r
#Text=account of its strong odour, resembling that of decayed onions.
#Text=On my\r
#Text=arrival in Batavia one of my first trips had been to the market to buy a\r
#Text=durian, which I brought to the hotel with anticipation of great enjoyment.
#Text=My disappointment was great, its taste being to me as offensive as its\r
#Text=odour.
#Text=Nobody knows what a durian is like until he eats one that has been\r
#Text=permitted to ripen and fall to the ground.
#Text=Even in Java this would be\r
#Text=difficult, unless one made special arrangements with the natives who bring\r
#Text=them to the market-places.
#Text=It is popularly supposed that the durian is an\r
#Text=aphrodisiac, but that is not the case.
#Text=Any food or fruit that one greatly\r
#Text=enjoys acts favourably on the digestive organs, and therefore makes one\r
#Text=feel in vigorous condition.
#Text=Those that were brought to me on this occasion, and which had just fallen\r
#Text=from the tree, were of a fresh green colour with a streak of yellow here\r
#Text=and there and had a pleasant, rich odour.
#Text=The most satisfactory way to eat\r
#Text=it is with a spoon; the pulp, though rich, is not heavy, and, moreover, is\r
#Text=stimulating.
#Text=It serves the purpose of a dessert, with a flavour and\r
#Text=delicacy that is indescribable and that makes one feel happy.
#Text=Among the\r
#Text=great enjoyments of life are the various delicious fruits when really ripe\r
#Text=and of the best grade, but comparatively few people have that experience.
#Text=The vast majority are perfectly satisfied to eat fruit that was picked\r
#Text=green and matured afterward.
#Text=Many years ago I tasted a real orange from\r
#Text=New-South-Wales, and ever since I have disdained the more acid kind.
#Text=The vaccinateur started in advance of us to prepare the people for our\r
#Text=arrival.
#Text=Our new paddlers, who were jolly and diligent men, brought their\r
#Text=rice packed in palm-leaves, one parcel for the men of each prahu.
#Text=They use\r
#Text=leaves of the banana even more frequently for such purposes, as also do\r
#Text=Javanese and Dayaks, and spread on the ground they form a neat and\r
#Text=inviting setting for the food, serving the purpose of a fresh table-cloth.
#Text=The men ate rapidly with their fingers and afterward drank water from the\r
#Text=kali (river), throwing it into the mouth with the hand, as is the Malay\r
#Text=custom.
#Text=I did not notice that they brought dried fish, which is the usual\r
#Text=complement to a meal.
#Text=In this section of the country there is much\r
#Text=admixture of blood between Dayaks and Malays, which accounts for the fact\r
#Text=that the latter are more genial and agreeable than their lower classes\r
#Text=usually are.
#Text=At Pinang the small population turned out in full force,\r
#Text=standing picturesquely near the mosque on an open space between the\r
#Text=cocoanut-trees that grew on the high river-bank.
#Text=It was evident that\r
#Text=visitors are not often seen there.
#Text=At Belimbing the usually steep, high river-bank had been made accessible\r
#Text=by short sticks so placed as to form steps that led up almost\r
#Text=perpendicularly.
#Text=Great was my surprise to find myself facing an attractive\r
#Text=little pasang-grahan, lying on grassy, level ground at almost the same\r
#Text=height as the tops of the cocoanut and pinang palms on the other side of\r
#Text=the river.
#Text=It was a lovely place and charmingly fresh and green.
#Text=The\r
#Text=house, neatly built of palm-leaves, contained two rooms and a small\r
#Text=kitchen, with floors of bamboo.
#Text=In the outer room was a table covered with\r
#Text=a red cloth and a lamp hung above it, for the Malays love the accessories\r
#Text=of civilisation.
#Text=The kapala and the vaccinateur were there to receive us,\r
#Text=and we were treated as if we were officials, two men sleeping in the house\r
#Text=as guard.
#Text=I was told there are no diseases here except mild cases of demum\r
#Text=(malaria) and an itching disorder of the skin between the fingers.
#Text=Nobody who has travelled in Borneo can have failed to notice the great\r
#Text=number of short-tailed cats.
#Text=In Bandjermasin those with long tails are\r
#Text=very rare, and among Malays and Dayaks I do not remember ever having seen\r
#Text=them.
#Text=They are either stub-tailed or they have a ball at the end of a tail\r
#Text=that is usually twisted and exceptionally short.
#Text=These cats are small and\r
#Text=extremely tame, and can hardly be pushed away with a kick, because they\r
#Text=have always been used to having their own way in the house.
#Text=They are more\r
#Text=resourceful and enterprising than the ordinary domestic cat, using their\r
#Text=claws to an almost incredible extent in climbing down perpendicular wooden\r
#Text=walls, or in running under the roof on rafters chasing mice.
#Text=I have twice\r
#Text=photographed such cats, a liberty which they resented by striking\r
#Text=viciously at the man who held them and growling all the time.
#Text=Their\r
#Text=accustomed food is rice and dried fish.
